# WSL

Working in WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux). This skill covers the interop
boundary, the filesystem and performance traps, and the shell-initialization
gotcha that makes tools behave differently in scripts than in your terminal. The
wsl.exe management and interop commands are in
[references/commands.md](references/commands.md).

## Interop and PATH

- WSL puts Windows executables on your Linux `PATH` (interop). `git`, `node`, or
  `python` can silently resolve to a Windows `.exe` under `/mnt/c/...` instead of
  the Linux one — check with `command -v <tool>` when behavior is surprising.
- Prefer the Linux-native toolchain for repo work. Reach for `foo.exe` only when
  you specifically want the Windows binary (e.g. `explorer.exe .`).

## Non-interactive vs login shells (the subtle one)

- A login or interactive shell sources your profile (`~/.bashrc` / `~/.zshrc`),
  which loads version managers and sets `PATH`. A script, a background job, or a
  hook run by a headless agent runs *non-interactively* and skips that profile —
  so the `PATH`, aliases, and version managers you rely on interactively are gone.
- Consequence: a command that works when you type it can fail from a script. Run
  it through a login shell (`bash -lc '...'`) or set the `PATH` you need
  explicitly at the top of the script. The node/nvm form of this trap — where
  `npx`/`node` resolve to a Windows install and break node-based hooks — lives in
  the `node` skill.

## Filesystem and performance

- Keep project files on the Linux filesystem (`~/...`), not `/mnt/c/...`: cross-OS
  `/mnt` access is far slower and carries different case-sensitivity and
  permission semantics.
- Keep LF line endings in the repo; a Windows editor writing CRLF into a Linux
  checkout breaks shell scripts and git hooks.
